To the humbly devout woman of Samaria who expected the Messiah, Jesus quietly disclosed, “I that speak unto thee am he” (John 4:26). Yet an anxious Pilate “saith unto Jesus, Whence art thou? But Jesus gave him no answer” (John 19:9). (Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Apr. 1989, 78; or Ensign, May 1989, 64)

“…an anxious Pilate ‘saith unto Jesus, Whence art thou? But Jesus gave him no answer.’ (John 19:9.) “Can we remain silent when silence is eloquence—but may be used against us? "Smoking Flax Shall He Not Quench." (Matt. 12:20.) Early Christians saw in Jesus the fulfillment of Isaiah's visions of the Suffering Servant. Isaiah 42 begins with the prophet speaking messianically, describing him as the epitome of gentle tenderness, unwilling to harm even the weakest plant. "A bruised reed shall he not break, and the smoking flax shall he not quench." (V. 3; see Matt. 12:20.)…
